what_it_does: Renders a standalone HTML design proof for a surface navigation rail, showing a horizontal rail mounted at the top and a vertical side-rail variant inside a framed demo area.
observations: Loads Plus Jakarta Sans and IBM Plex Mono from Google Fonts.
Uses shared styles from /surface/styles/piece.css and /surface/styles/phone.css plus small inline demo styles.
Mounts one nav rail into #top with active surface set to decisions.
Mounts a vertical nav rail into #vside with active surface set to channels.
Defines five surface items: Decisions, The V, Channels, Board, and Transcript.
Depends on /surface/runtime/surface.js, dna-icons.js, icons.js, and organisms.js for rendering.
standouts: The file demonstrates both horizontal and vertical variants of the same navigation organism.
Active state is illustrated with different selected surfaces in each variant.
The demo copy frames the rail as navigation between operator-facing surfaces.
conventions: Uses a standalone proof-page HTML structure with inline setup script.
Uses DNA.org.navRail as the rendering entry point.
Uses data objects with id, label, and icon fields for navigation items.
Mixes shared CSS files with file-local inline CSS for the demo shell.
concerns: The horizontal rail caption appears after the #top mount rather than before or around the rendered rail.
The file relies on absolute /surface paths, so it expects a specific served URL context.
Inline style on the second caption duplicates spacing concerns inside markup.
notes: This is a focused visual proof page for the surface nav rail organism and its vertical variant.
questions: Should the horizontal caption be visually associated with the #top rail, or is the current order intentional?
Are the icon names guaranteed by the loaded runtime icon registries?
Is the placeholder projection text meant to remain visible in this proof page?
purpose_vs_actual: The title and captions describe a surface nav rail design proof, and the file directly renders horizontal and vertical nav rail examples.
intention_signals: It seems intended to validate the nav rail's appearance and active-state behavior in both top and side placements using representative surface destinations.
novelty: The page treats navigation as a set of named operating surfaces and demonstrates orientation switching through the same organism call.
contribution: Provides a compact nav-rail substrate with surface item metadata, active-state examples, and horizontal/vertical presentation patterns that a fused system would keep as a navigation proof and component fixture.
summary_for_embedding: Standalone HTML proof page that renders a DNA.org.navRail component with five surface destinations in horizontal and vertical configurations.
intention_for_embedding: This file was for inspecting and communicating the surface navigation rail as an operator-facing between-surface control.
